title: Britain’s next generation is all grown up
gist: A data-dense survey of UK young adults finds them squeezed by housing and job-market conditions and stuck in a prolonged 'delayed adulthood,' yet contrary to the stereotype of apathy or radicalisation, they are highly politically engaged and prioritise the same bread-and-butter economic issues as older generations — with fixes for youth disengagement likely lying in fixing the economy, not youth-specific gimmicks.

insights.claim: Almost 3.8m families in England and Wales had adult children living with them in 2021, a rise of 13.6 per cent over the preceding decade, reflecting a structural shift toward young adults staying in the parental home far longer than previous generations.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: A durable demand signal against independent-household formation, with knock-on effects for rental and starter-home demand, family spending patterns, and intergenerational wealth transfer needs.
insights.locator: As the last census found

insights.claim: Homeownership among 25- to 34-year-olds in England fell from 59 per cent in 2000 to only 39 per cent in 2022, according to the Institute for Fiscal Studies — a two-decade collapse in this cohort's ability to buy property.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Confirms a multi-decade structural decline in first-time-buyer homeownership that policy and housebuilding have not reversed — relevant to UK housing/mortgage and build-to-rent investment theses.
insights.locator: According to the Institute for Fiscal Studies thinktank

insights.claim: Private renters in the UK spent on average just over 36 per cent of gross income on rent in 2024 — above the 30 per cent threshold the ONS treats as the affordability ceiling — and for renters aged 16 to 24 specifically, the government's English Housing Survey put the share at 46 per cent.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Quantifies acute rent-burden concentration among the youngest renters, a segment with the least bargaining power and savings buffer — a risk factor for consumer spending and household formation.
insights.locator: Though the Office for National Statistics considers rent to be affordable

insights.claim: 735,000 people aged 16-24 were unemployed in the UK as of early 2026, a year-on-year rise of over 100,000, with the youth unemployment rate climbing from 14.3 per cent to 16.2 per cent over the same period; in 2026 the UK also passed one million NEETs (not in education, employment or training) for the first time since 2003.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: A sharp, fast-moving deterioration in youth labour-market conditions is a leading indicator worth tracking against UK consumer and political-risk models; the 1m-NEET threshold is a symbolically important milestone likely to draw policy attention.
insights.locator: Elsewhere, figures from earlier this year showed

insights.claim: The UK ranked 32nd for youth wellbeing in the most recent World Happiness Report versus 20th for its overall population, and a separate Children's Society study found the UK's 15-year-olds to be the least happy of 15-year-olds across 27 European countries.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: The UK has a specific, comparatively severe youth-wellbeing deficit relative to peer countries, not just a generic global-youth malaise — suggests domestic policy factors rather than only global trends are driving the gap.
insights.locator: Really, it shouldn't be a surprise that in the most recent World Happiness Report

insights.claim: One in four young people in the UK has a common mental health condition according to an NHS survey, a figure that rose by over a third in a decade; the top self-reported contributors to youth anxiety are financial worries (37 per cent), work pressure (23 per cent) and job insecurity or unemployment (20 per cent).
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Mental-health strain among young people is predominantly economic in origin rather than purely clinical/cultural, implying that labour-market and cost-of-living interventions would have outsized wellbeing payoff for this cohort.
insights.locator: an NHS survey conducted last year found that one in four young people

themes.theme: The myth of youth political apathy

insights.claim: IPPR researcher Chris Bick found that when political engagement is measured broadly — talking about politics, attending protests, signing petitions — rather than narrowly by voting or party membership, young people are remarkably engaged, probably more so than older generations; only 26 per cent of survey respondents in the UK Youth Poll hadn't taken part in any political activity in the past year.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Media and political narratives of youth apathy are measurement artefacts, not reality — parties writing off youth engagement, or targeting it only with gimmicks, are misreading a genuinely mobilisable electorate.
insights.quote: The thing that really surprised me was how much they care about politics.
insights.locator: Before we really jumped into this research

insights.claim: According to the John Smith Centre's UK Youth Poll, 59 per cent of 16-29 year-olds agree that politics is an honourable job (versus 28 per cent disagreeing) and about 65 per cent believe the political system is broken, yet young people are less likely than older generations to say voting doesn't matter.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Young voters combine high normative respect for democratic participation with high dissatisfaction with current institutions — a combustible mix that could translate into support for reformist or insurgent parties rather than disengagement.
insights.locator: As his team found, 59 per cent of them agree

insights.claim: Both the UK Youth Poll and a Department for Education survey of 14-24 year-olds found young people's top-ranked issues to be inflation/cost of living, healthcare/NHS, housing affordability and mental health — essentially the same priority ranking as the general population — while only 12 per cent cited climate change as a top issue (two points below the general public) and Gaza and trans issues were each cited by under 5 per cent.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Directly undercuts the 'culture war' framing of Gen Z politics — parties courting young voters with identity/climate messaging over economic policy (housing, wages, cost of living) are targeting the wrong priorities.
insights.quote: This is not a group of people who are banging the culture wars drum. They are very, very concerned by bread-and-butter issues.
insights.locator: As Greenwood-Hau concludes while discussing the topic more broadly

themes.theme: 'Delayed adulthood' and the misuse of brain science

insights.claim: Bobby Duffy (King's College London) argues that today's prolonged transition to adulthood is better described as 'delayed adulthood' rather than Jeffrey Arnett's 2000 concept of 'emerging adulthood' — for many young people it is not a chosen period of exploration but a frustrating inability to reach traditional adult milestones (home, career, independence).
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Reframes a common demographic narrative: policies assuming young adults are voluntarily delaying milestones (and thus need no urgent intervention) misread a cohort that is structurally blocked, not choosing to wait.
insights.quote: It's not a wonderful time of exploration for some people, it's a frustration that they can't move on to those more adult years.
insights.locator: Bobby Duffy, who studies generations

insights.claim: Neuroscientist Dean Burnett pushes back on the popular claim (cited in Scottish sentencing guidance for under-25s, in campaigns against under-25/21 transgender transition, and in stigmatising age-gap relationships) that 'the brain isn't fully developed until 25,' arguing brain development stages don't map neatly onto cognitive ability or capacity to consent, and that individual variation is massive.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: A scientific claim is being used across law, healthcare and social discourse to justify extending legal/social 'teenagehood' well past 18 — worth scrutinising in any policy analysis citing '25 and the brain' as a rationale for age-based restrictions.
insights.quote: Even if there are specific stages of brain development that happen at essentially the same time in every individual, this doesn't map neatly onto cognitive abilities.
insights.locator: The prevalence of this idea keeps Dean Burnett up at night

insights.claim: Bobby Duffy's framework attributes generational differences to three separate forces: life-cycle effects (how people change as they age), period effects (shared shocks like pandemics, wars, economic crises), and cohort effects (how formative-year circumstances shape a generation differently from others) — useful for disentangling which youth trends are age-related versus structural.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: A reusable analytical lens for separating durable cohort-specific shifts (e.g. permanently lower homeownership) from temporary life-stage or one-off shock effects when forecasting future consumer/voter behaviour.
insights.locator: As Duffy puts it, three important things influence someone

insights.claim: Duffy warns that a large concentration of private wealth held by baby boomers and parts of Gen X will flow down to younger generations very unevenly, undermining generational solidarity because some young people receive substantial parental financial support while others receive none.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Points to widening intra-generational (not just inter-generational) wealth inequality among young adults as intergenerational transfers accelerate — a factor for wealth-management and inheritance-planning demand forecasts.
insights.locator: A huge concentration of private wealth

insights.claim: Duffy identifies a historically new phenomenon — that today's young people can no longer expect a better future than their parents — as a deep threat to social cohesion, because a felt loss of generational progress erodes belief that 'the system' works at all.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: A break in the expectation of generational progress is a structural political-risk factor (fuelling populism and institutional distrust) that outlasts any single election cycle.
insights.quote: the real threat of the lack of generational progress is more people thinking: 'Christ, the system doesn't work at all.'
insights.locator: Young people can no longer expect a better future than their parents

insights.claim: Contrary to media narratives of young men swinging heavily to Reform UK, IPPR research found young men are the most progressive male age cohort on every salient culture-war issue (from immigration to sexism), and the increase in Reform voting intention was lowest among men aged 18-25.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: The 'red-pilled young men' narrative driving some political strategy is empirically overstated — parties banking on a young-male shift to the populist right may be misallocating resources.
insights.locator: As the IPPR report found, young men are the most progressive

insights.claim: Oxford politics fellow Ceri Fowler attributes young women's leftward shift partly to compositional change — the proportion of women with a university degree has risen substantially across generations — combined with women's generally greater support (across all generations) for redistribution and state intervention, rather than to generational radicalisation.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Suggests the youth gender political gap is driven by durable structural factors (education composition, economic-values gender gap) rather than a fad, meaning it is likely to persist rather than reverse quickly.
insights.locator: Women in general—across generations—are more supportive than men

insights.claim: Fowler cites two recent Greater Manchester by-elections as evidence young left-leaning women are persuadable, not lost to Labour: in Gorton and Denton the left vote split with the Greens drawing younger women's support, while in Makerfield the left (including women) consolidated behind Labour's Andy Burnham against the Reform threat.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Labour has a plausible path to winning back younger progressive women with a mix of liberal cultural positioning and economic support for young people — this voter bloc is contestable, not structurally lost.
insights.locator: We can see this in the two by-elections in Manchester

insights.claim: Optimism about one's personal future is sharply divided by employment status: 70 per cent of full-time young workers were optimistic in 2025 versus only 44 per cent of those not in work; separately, an Electoral Commission study of 2,501 young people aged 11-25 found 67 per cent from the most affluent socioeconomic backgrounds (AB) were interested in politics, versus just 32 per cent from the least affluent (DE).
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Youth disengagement and pessimism are concentrated among the economically disadvantaged within the cohort, not evenly spread — targeted economic and civic-engagement interventions for lower-income young people would likely have the largest marginal effect.
insights.locator: You may remember the stat from earlier about how young people feel

insights.claim: The author proposes making voting compulsory for first-time voters in general elections (paired with the likely extension of the franchise to 16- and 17-year-olds), citing research that people who fail to vote in their first eligible election are much less likely to start voting later, and that voting is 'infectious' within households — a person is more likely to vote if another household member does.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: A low-cost policy lever (first-vote compulsion) could durably raise lifetime turnout and, via household spillover, even re-engage older non-voting relatives who now live with adult children for longer.
insights.locator: One idea is to make voting compulsory in general elections for first-time voters

insights.claim: Research from Scotland, Austria and some South American countries cited in the piece finds that early enfranchisement (voting before turning 18) correlates with higher long-term trust in political parties and higher likelihood of continued voting into adulthood.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Supports lowering the voting age as a durable pro-turnout, pro-institutional-trust policy, relevant to assessing the UK's planned extension of the franchise to 16-17 year-olds.
insights.locator: According to various pieces of research from Scotland, Austria

insights.claim: Lithuania was rated the country with the happiest young people in the 2024 World Happiness Report; the article attributes this to national GDP more than quadrupling within young Lithuanians' lifetimes, average gross earnings rising over 12 per cent in a single recent year, below-European-average rents, and largely free university education — not to any distinctly 'youth' policy.
insights.speaker: Marie Le Conte
insights.so_what: Reinforces the article's central thesis: youth wellbeing tracks aggregate macroeconomic performance (growth, wages, affordable housing, free education) rather than youth-targeted messaging — a cross-country natural experiment supporting broad economic fixes over demographic-specific gimmicks.
insights.quote: it really helps under-thirties if their home country's national GDP has more than quadrupled in their lifetime
insights.locator: This is especially obvious when looking at Lithuania
